as_of_note: Verified against official docs as fetched on the dates below. Docs change.
notes: Headline platform: capped at needs_human. Named-on-a-slide claims require human sign-off, not tool self-certification.
Additionally, 3 required step(s) are not backed by a quoted official-doc excerpt.

steps.action: Create or sign in to a personal GitHub account and complete required email verification.
steps.success_signal: The GitHub dashboard and New repository action are accessible.

evidence.excerpt: personal account Navigate to https://github.com/ . Click Sign up . Alternatively, click on Continue with Google to sign up using social login. Follow the prompts to create your personal account. During sign up, you'll be asked to verify your email address. Without a verified email address, you won't be able to complete some basic GitHub

GitHub essentials like repositories, branches, commits, and pull requests. You'll create your own Hello World repository and learn GitHub's pull request workflow, a popular way to create and review code. In this quickstart guide, you will: Create and use a repository. Start and manage a new

steps.action: Select New repository, set Repository name to hello-world, type a short description, select Public or Private, select Add a README file, and select Create repository.
steps.success_signal: The repository Code tab shows README.md on main.

evidence.excerpt: select , then click New repository . In the "Repository name" box, type hello-world . In the "Description" box, type a short description. For example, type "This repository is for practicing the GitHub Flow." Select whether your repository will be Public or Private . Select Add a README file . Click Create

main branch while you were working on your branch, you could pull in those updates. steps.action: Open the branch dropdown from main, type readme-edits, and select Create branch: readme-edits from main.
steps.success_signal: The repository branch selector shows readme-edits.

evidence.excerpt: branch called feature The journey that feature takes through stages for "Commit changes," "Submit pull request," and "Discuss proposed changes" before it's merged into main Creating a branch Click the Code tab of your hello-world repository. Above the file list, click the dropdown menu that says main . Type a branch name, readme-edits , into the text box. Click Create

steps.action: On readme-edits, open README.md, select Edit, and write a short description about yourself.
steps.success_signal: The editor diff shows the intended addition on readme-edits.
notes: No literal official-doc excerpt supports the step's success_signal.

evidence.excerpt: description explaining why a particular change was made. Commit messages capture the history of your changes so that other contributors can understand what you’ve done and why. Under the readme-edits branch you created, click the README.md file. To edit the file, click . In the editor, write a bit about yourself

shows: The main branch A new branch called feature The journey that feature takes through stages for "Commit changes," "Submit pull request," and "Discuss proposed changes" before it's merged into main Creating a branch Click the Code tab of your hello-world repository. Above the file list, click the dropdown menu that says main . Type a branch name, readme-edits

steps.action: Select Commit changes, enter a descriptive commit message, and select Commit changes again.
steps.success_signal: The branch contains a commit and differs from main.
notes: No literal official-doc excerpt supports the step's action.

evidence.excerpt: commit changes When you created a new branch in the previous step, GitHub brought you to the code page for your new readme-edits branch, which is a copy of main . You can make and save changes to the files in your repository. On GitHub, saved changes are called commits. Each commit has an associated commit message

Commit changes . In the "Commit changes" box, write a commit message that describes your changes. Click Commit changes . These changes will be made only to the README file on your readme-edits branch, so now this branch contains content that's different from main

steps.action: Open Pull requests, select New pull request, compare readme-edits against main, and review the diff.
steps.success_signal: The compare page shows the README additions and no unintended changes.

evidence.excerpt: open a pull request in your own repository and then merge it yourself. It's a great way to practice the GitHub flow before working on larger projects. Click the Pull requests tab of your hello-world repository. Click New pull request . In the Example Comparisons box, select the branch you made, readme-edits , to compare with main
readme-edits , to compare with main (the original). Look over your changes in the diffs on the Compare page

steps.action: Select Create pull request, add a title and brief description, and select Create pull request again.
steps.success_signal: The pull request Conversation page is open with the expected diff.
notes: No literal official-doc excerpt supports the step's success_signal.

evidence.excerpt: pull request . In the Example Comparisons box, select the branch you made, readme-edits , to compare with main (the original). Look over your changes in the diffs on the Compare page, make sure they're what you want to submit. Click Create pull request . Give your pull request a title and write a brief description

steps.action: At the bottom of the pull request, select Merge pull request and then Confirm merge.
steps.success_signal: GitHub reports the pull request was successfully merged and closed.

evidence.excerpt: pull request to discuss the conflicts with your team members. In this walk-through, you should not have any conflicts, so you are ready to merge your branch into the main branch. At the bottom of the pull request, click Merge pull request to merge the changes into main . Click Confirm
pull request, click Merge pull request to merge the changes into main . Click Confirm merge . You will receive a message that the request was successfully merged and the request was closed
